findings.description: Session tokens are generated with random.choice() (Mersenne Twister PRNG), which is not cryptographically secure and is state-recoverable/predictable, allowing an attacker to forge or predict other users' session tokens (CWE-338/CWE-330, OWASP A07/A02); use the secrets module instead.

Importing and relying on the `random` module as the entropy source for authentication-bearer tokens is the root cause of the weak-token vulnerability; replace with `secrets` / os.urandom-backed generation.

touch() slides expiry forward without checking expires_at or purging, so an expired-but-unpurged session can be resurrected and kept alive indefinitely, bypassing the TTL session-expiration control (CWE-613).

create() enforces no cap on total or per-user sessions and purging only removes already-expired entries, so a flood of create() calls within the TTL window grows _sessions without bound (unbounded memory/DoS, CWE-770).

user_sessions() neither purges nor filters on expires_at, so it can return already-expired sessions; if a caller uses the result for 'is this user logged in'/authorization decisions, expired sessions are treated as active (expiry bypass, CWE-613).

Token lookup uses a non-constant-time dict.get comparison; theoretically a timing side channel but not practically exploitable for 32-char high-entropy keys (reported for completeness per exhaustive-reporting mandate).
No security/audit logging of session lifecycle events (create/destroy/expire); the declared logger is never used, leaving no audit trail for session hijacking investigation (CWE-778, informational).
